note: GENERATED by scripts/build-persona-data.mjs from research.json — do not hand-edit; edit research.json and re-run. Photos merge at load time from photos.json.

personas.name: Paul Morphy
personas.bio: A New Orleans prodigy who crushed the American field at 20, then toured Europe in 1858-59 and demolished every master who would face him, including Anderssen. Widely considered the first truly modern player for his understanding of development and open lines. He retired from chess at 22 and never played seriously again.
personas.title: GM
personas.modernEloNote: Expert consensus puts Morphy around 2350-2550 against today's field: his open-game tactics and development principles were a century ahead, but he never faced modern defensive technique and knows none of 165 years of opening theory - 2475 splits that range honestly.
personas.styleDesc: Morphy plays 1.e4 and races every piece into play before striking - Evans Gambits, King's Gambits and the Ruy Lopez, always aiming at f7 and the uncastled king. His sacrifices are not wild gambles but the logical payoff of a lead in development and open lines. Endgames are handled cleanly but he would rather never reach one.

personas.name: Adolf Anderssen
personas.bio: A Breslau mathematics teacher who became the world's leading player by winning London 1851, the first international tournament, and London 1862. His Immortal and Evergreen games are the two most celebrated attacking miniatures in chess history. He was defeated decisively by Morphy in 1858 and by Steinitz in 1866, bookending the Romantic era.
personas.title: GM
personas.modernEloNote: The greatest pure attacker of the Romantic era, but his all-gambit repertoire and casual defense would be ruthlessly refuted by anyone with modern technique - roughly FM strength today.
personas.styleDesc: Anderssen plays gambit chess in its purest form: King's Gambits and Evans Gambits, pawns and pieces thrown at the enemy king for open lines. He calculates mating combinations better than anyone of his generation and will sacrifice both rooks and the queen if a mate is at the end of it. Defense and endgames interest him only as a last resort.

personas.name: Wilhelm Steinitz
personas.bio: The first official World Champion (1886-1894) and the father of positional chess. He overturned the Romantic dogma that attack was mandatory, proving that defense and pawn structure win games, and defended his theories in print as fiercely as over the board. Born in Prague, he spent his later career in New York and died in poverty.
personas.title: GM
personas.modernEloNote: The inventor of positional chess would grasp modern ideas faster than any Romantic, but his cramped defensive setups and king walks are known refuted territory today - around 2450 against current opposition.
personas.styleDesc: Steinitz plays for the accumulation of small advantages: the bishop pair, better pawn structure, a safe king. He is happy to defend cramped but sound positions, believing an attack is only justified when the position demands it - and when it is, he executes it with Romantic-era sharpness. His openings favor slow d3/c3 Ruy Lopez structures and his own gambit lines where the king marches up the board.

personas.name: Emanuel Lasker
personas.bio: World Champion for 27 years (1894-1921), the longest reign ever, defeating Steinitz and turning back Marshall, Tarrasch, Janowski and Schlechter. A mathematician (the Lasker-Noether theorem bears his name) and philosopher who treated chess as a struggle between imperfect humans. He fled Nazi Germany in 1933 and finished his life in New York.
personas.title: GM
personas.modernEloNote: Lasker's psychological pragmatism, defensive resourcefulness and fighting endgames age far better than his era's openings; he would be a dangerous 2600+ grandmaster today.
personas.styleDesc: Lasker plays the man, not the board: he steers toward positions his opponent dislikes, accepts objectively dubious but practically poisonous complications, and defends worse positions with legendary tenacity. His openings are simple - Ruy Lopez (including the Exchange) and classical Queen's Gambit lines - because the fight starts in the middlegame. He is arguably history's greatest counterattacker and swindler.

personas.name: José Raúl Capablanca
personas.bio: Havana-born third World Champion (1921-1927), nicknamed 'the Human Chess Machine'. He learned the moves at four by watching his father, beat Cuba's champion at twelve, and took the title from Lasker without losing a game. His natural, technique-first style became the model for Fischer, Karpov and Carlsen.
personas.title: GM
personas.modernEloNote: Capablanca's positional intuition and endgame technique are essentially timeless - only the opening-preparation gap keeps 'the chess machine' below today's elite at around 2680.
personas.styleDesc: Capablanca plays with effortless clarity: quick development, sound structures, exchanges into favorable endgames that he converts with legendary precision. He avoids unnecessary risk almost entirely - he went eight years (1916-1924) without losing a single serious game. His Ruy Lopez and Queen's Gambit repertoires are narrow but bulletproof, and when attacked he defends with cold, exact calculation.

personas.name: Alexander Alekhine
personas.bio: Fourth World Champion (1927-1935, 1937-1946), the only champion to die holding the title. A Moscow aristocrat who fled the revolution to France, he dethroned the 'invincible' Capablanca in 1927 through sheer preparation and will. His collections of best games are still considered among the finest annotation work ever written.
personas.title: GM
personas.modernEloNote: Alekhine's deep combinative vision and pioneering preparation would translate well, but modern defenders neutralize speculative attacks that his contemporaries could not - about 2650 today.
personas.styleDesc: Alekhine builds dynamic, tension-filled positions and then detonates them with combinations calculated further than anyone of his era. He prepares openings deeply - a novelty in his day - playing both 1.e4 and 1.d4, and as Black his own Alekhine Defense and sharp Slav lines. He wants the initiative at almost any cost and famously said he had to 'defeat the position' before defeating the opponent.

personas.name: Akiba Rubinstein
personas.bio: A rabbinical student from Stawiski, Poland, who taught himself chess at 16 and by 1912 - winning five consecutive major tournaments - was the uncrowned world champion; World War I destroyed his negotiated title match with Lasker. Increasingly crippled by anthropophobia, he would hide in the corner of the hall after making each move. He retired in 1932 and survived the Holocaust hidden in a Brussels sanatorium.
personas.title: GM
personas.modernEloNote: His rook endgames and harmonious opening systems (many still main lines bearing his name) hold up remarkably, but nerves and a narrow attacking arsenal cap him around 2570 against modern opposition.
personas.styleDesc: Rubinstein plays chess as a single unified plan: openings chosen so every piece points at the endgame he intends to reach. His Queen's Gambit systems and the Rubinstein French are models of harmony, and his rook endgames are still the textbook standard - Spielmann called them witchcraft. He attacks rarely, but when the position demands it he produces combinations as pure as his Immortal at Lodz.

personas.name: Mikhail Botvinnik
personas.bio: The 'Patriarch' of the Soviet chess school, World Champion 1948-57, 1958-60 and 1961-63. An electrical engineer by profession, he treated chess as a science - hygiene, preparation, and rigorous post-mortems - and his school later trained Karpov, Kasparov and Kramnik. He was the first great champion to take computer chess seriously, spending his retirement on chess algorithms.
personas.title: GM
personas.modernEloNote: Botvinnik invented the systematic preparation, physical training and adjournment analysis that define modern professional chess - the smallest theory gap of any pre-computer champion puts him around 2720 today.
personas.styleDesc: Botvinnik plays 'scientific' chess: deeply researched opening systems (his Semi-Slav Botvinnik System, French Winawer and English structures) steering into strategically complex middlegames he has studied at home. He willingly accepts static weaknesses for dynamic central control and grinds opponents down with iron logic rather than flash. A ferocious match player who twice won back the world title in revenge matches.

personas.name: Mikhail Tal
personas.bio: Youngest world champion of his time, dethroning Botvinnik in 1960 at age 23 with an avalanche of sacrifices. Chronic kidney illness cut short his reign and dogged his career, yet he remained a top-10 player for three decades and set a record 95-game unbeaten streak in 1973-74. Universally beloved for his wit, chain-smoking bravado, and pure love of the game.
personas.title: GM
personas.modernEloNote: His calculation and initiative would still beat most 2700s, but engine-checked defenders refute many of his speculative sacrifices, and his fragile health limited consistency — roughly 2700 against today's field.
personas.styleDesc: The 'Magician from Riga' sacrificed pieces for initiative and attack, dragging opponents into irrational complications where he calculated better than anyone alive. He played open Sicilians and the Ruy Lopez with White, and the King's Indian and Modern Benoni with Black. Endgames and quiet positions bored him; he steered every game toward chaos.

personas.name: Tigran Petrosian
personas.bio: 'Iron Tigran', world champion 1963-1969, dethroned Botvinnik and defended against Spassky in 1966 before losing the rematch. An Armenian national hero, he redefined defense and prophylaxis as weapons and made the positional exchange sacrifice his personal trademark. Away from the board he was a candidate master at... backgammon jokes aside, an editor and beloved figure of Soviet chess.
personas.title: GM
personas.modernEloNote: Prophylaxis and defensive technique are exactly what engines vindicated, so his core skill ages well; a modest opening-theory gap and low ambition with White keep him near 2680 rather than higher.
personas.styleDesc: The supreme prophylactic player: he saw opponents' plans before they did and quietly strangled them, famously trading rook for minor piece in positional exchange sacrifices. With White he favored slow d4/c4 and flank systems (the Petrosian System vs the King's Indian, a3 vs the Queen's Indian); with Black the French, Caro-Kann and King's Indian. Almost impossible to beat — he lost fewer games than any champion of his era.

personas.name: Vasily Smyslov
personas.bio: Seventh world champion (1957-58), he contested three straight title matches with Botvinnik and scored better than the patriarch across all 69 games. A trained baritone who nearly chose opera over chess, he reached the Candidates final again in 1984 at age 63 — the oldest finalist ever — losing only to young Kasparov. His career at the top spanned six decades.
personas.title: GM
personas.modernEloNote: His harmony-based positional play and world-class endgame technique translate directly, but his pre-computer opening approach concedes ground to modern prep, landing him around 2670 today.
personas.styleDesc: Played by 'the hand's intuition' — smooth, harmonious piece placement, small accumulating advantages, and arguably the finest endgame technique of any world champion. With White he alternated the closed Ruy Lopez, English and Queen's Gambit; with Black his name is attached to variations of the Grünfeld, Slav, Caro-Kann and Ruy Lopez. Never forced matters, but punished the slightest disharmony.

personas.name: Boris Spassky
personas.bio: Tenth world champion (1969-1972), he beat Petrosian at the second attempt and then lost the 'Match of the Century' to Fischer in Reykjavik 1972 — the most famous chess match ever played. Charismatic and sportsmanlike (he applauded Fischer's game-6 masterpiece from the board), he emigrated to France in 1976 and remained a top player into the 1980s.
personas.title: GM
personas.modernEloNote: The first truly universal champion — equally at home attacking or grinding — a style that ports cleanly to modern chess, though his famously relaxed attitude to preparation would cost him against today's prep machines.
personas.styleDesc: The first 'universal player': he could sacrifice like Tal one day and squeeze like Petrosian the next, with a special gift for harmonious middlegame attacks. With White he mixed the Ruy Lopez, Closed Sicilian and even the King's Gambit at top level; with Black he trusted the Breyer Ruy Lopez and Tartakower Queen's Gambit. His intuition for the initiative in the middlegame was second to none.

personas.name: Bobby Fischer
personas.bio: Eleventh world champion, the American who single-handedly broke Soviet hegemony by beating Taimanov and Larsen 6-0 each, then Spassky in Reykjavik 1972 at the height of the Cold War. US champion at 14, grandmaster at 15, author of the 11/11 US Championship and 'My 60 Memorable Games'. He forfeited the title in 1975 and vanished into a troubled seclusion, but his games remain a model of clarity.
personas.title: GM
personas.modernEloNote: His engine-like accuracy, monomaniacal work ethic and 1970-72 dominance (125 points clear of No. 2) suggest he would absorb modern theory faster than anyone and remain a top-5 player, around 2770.
personas.styleDesc: Crystal-clear, ferociously precise chess: he took what the position offered — attack, endgame, or technique — and converted with machine-like accuracy. With White it was 1.e4 ('best by test'): the Ruy Lopez, the Exchange Ruy he revived, and the Fischer-Sozin Bc4 against the Najdorf. With Black he played the Najdorf (including the Poisoned Pawn) and King's Indian almost exclusively, and his repertoire, though narrow, was analyzed deeper than anyone's.

personas.name: Anatoly Karpov
personas.bio: Twelfth world champion (1975-1985), he earned the crown when Fischer forfeited, then silenced doubters by dominating tournament chess for a decade and winning over 160 first prizes — the most of any player. His five title matches with Kasparov (1984-1990) are the fiercest rivalry in chess history, and his Linares 1994 victory (11/13) is often called the greatest tournament performance ever.
personas.title: GM
personas.modernEloNote: His positional squeeze and conversion technique are timeless and he already proved himself into the mid-1990s (Linares 1994 at 2985 performance); slower calculation late in games versus modern pace costs him a little, around 2740.
personas.styleDesc: The boa constrictor: tiny improvements, total piece coordination, and merciless exploitation of one weakness — his opponents often resigned without knowing where they went wrong. With White he squeezed via the Ruy Lopez and later the Queen's Gambit and Nimzo with e3; with Black his Caro-Kann (4...Nd7 Karpov Variation), Zaitsev Ruy Lopez and Queen's Indian were nearly airtight. Rarely sacrificed, never bluffed, converted endgame edges like a machine.

personas.name: Garry Kasparov
personas.bio: Thirteenth world champion (1985-2000) and the youngest ever at 22, he won five brutal matches against Karpov and stayed world No. 1 for two decades until retiring in 2005. His 1997 loss to Deep Blue marked a turning point for chess and AI, and his 2851 rating stood as the all-time record until Carlsen. Now a writer, coach and political activist.
personas.title: GM
personas.modernEloNote: He pioneered computer-assisted preparation and held the No. 1 spot for 20 years ending in 2005 — already effectively a modern player; only Carlsen-era endgame grinding and today's depth of prep shave a few points off his 2851 peak.
personas.styleDesc: Dynamic energy incarnate: deep opening preparation converted into raging initiative, pawn sacrifices for activity, and attacks calculated to the end. With White he wielded razor-sharp theory in the Najdorf English Attack, the Grünfeld Exchange and his revived Scotch; with Black the Najdorf/Scheveningen Sicilian, King's Indian and Grünfeld. He treated the opening as a weapon of intimidation and outworked every rival.

personas.name: Judit Polgar
personas.bio: The strongest female player in history by a huge margin, she refused women-only events, became a grandmaster at 15 (breaking Fischer's record) and reached world No. 8 with wins over eleven world champions, including Kasparov, Anand, Carlsen and Topalov. Raised in the famous Polgar sisters experiment in Budapest, she played in the male World Championship cycle and retired in 2014 to focus on chess education.
personas.title: GM
personas.modernEloNote: She peaked at world No. 8 in the engine era itself and beat Kasparov, Carlsen, Anand and Topalov in her prime, so her strength barely discounts — around 2710 against today's field.
personas.styleDesc: Uncompromising attacking chess built on ferocious tactics: she played 1.e4 virtually every game, dove into the sharpest open Sicilians, and hunted kings with sacrifices that stood up to engine scrutiny. With Black she trusted the Najdorf and King's Indian. Her motto was to always play for a win — draws by agreement barely existed in her games.

personas.name: Viswanathan Anand
personas.bio: The 15th World Champion (2007-2013) and India's first grandmaster, 'Vishy' won world titles in three different formats and made Chennai a chess capital. A five-time World Champion match winner known as the 'Lightning Kid' for his speed, he remained a top-10 player across four decades.
personas.title: GM
personas.modernEloNote: Still rated ~2740-2750 and dangerous in his mid-50s, but slightly below the absolute elite he once led; rapid speed of calculation remains world class.
personas.styleDesc: Lightning-fast calculator who wins with classical piece activity: Ruy Lopez and open Sicilians with White, Najdorf and Meran counterattacks with Black. Famous for deep opening preparation that erupts into direct kingside attacks, and for converting initiative into a win faster than almost anyone alive. Comfortable in every phase, but at his best when the position turns sharp and concrete.

personas.name: Vladimir Kramnik
personas.bio: The 14th World Champion, who beat Garry Kasparov in London 2000 — the only man to defeat Kasparov in a match — and held the classical title until 2007. Renowned as one of the deepest strategists in chess history and, since retiring, an influential (and provocative) commentator and coach.
personas.title: GM
personas.modernEloNote: Retired from classical chess in 2019; still plays elite-level rapid and online chess but classical rust and age put him a tier below today's top ten.
personas.styleDesc: The great neutralizer: deep positional strategy, iron-clad structures and legendary endgame technique. Built the Berlin Wall that dethroned Kasparov, and with White squeezes wins from the Catalan, the Bb2/Nf3 systems and dry Nimzo lines where nothing seems to be happening until it is lost for the opponent. In his later career he paradoxically became one of the most creative attacking experimenters in the elite.

personas.name: Magnus Carlsen
personas.bio: The 16th World Champion (2013-2023), five-time title match winner and holder of the highest rating in history (2882). Widely considered the greatest player of all time, he gave up the classical title in 2023 out of boredom and continues to dominate every format, especially rapid and blitz.
personas.title: GM
personas.modernEloNote: World No. 1 at 2841 on the June 2026 FIDE list — still clearly the strongest player alive despite abandoning the world championship cycle.
personas.styleDesc: The universal grinder: takes dry, 'equal' positions and squeezes water from stone with peerless endgame technique and zero-mistake pressure chess. Opening choices are deliberately varied and modest — quiet Italians, anti-Berlins, sidelines in every system — because he wants a playable middlegame, not a theory duel. When opponents overreach he punishes with sudden, exact tactics.

personas.name: Hikaru Nakamura
personas.bio: Five-time US Champion, longtime world No. 2-3, and the dominant online blitz player of two decades. Reinvented himself as a streaming superstar with millions of followers while somehow keeping his classical rating at the very top, qualifying for multiple Candidates tournaments in his late 30s.
personas.title: GM
personas.modernEloNote: World No. 3 at 2792 on the June 2026 FIDE list despite streaming full-time — arguably the best speed-chess player in history.
personas.styleDesc: Fearless tactical fighter raised on blitz: razor-sharp calculation, swindling defense, and total comfort in chaos. His King's Indian Defense wins are legendary — he willingly cedes space to launch all-out kingside pawn storms — while with White he keeps flexible Ruy/English setups and outplays people in mutual time pressure. The greatest practical player of his generation.

personas.name: Fabiano Caruana
personas.bio: American superstar who ran up a legendary 7/7 start at the 2014 Sinquefield Cup (a 3080 performance, the best tournament run ever) and challenged Carlsen for the world title in 2018, drawing all 12 classical games. Multiple-time US Champion and perennial Candidates contender.
personas.title: GM
personas.modernEloNote: World No. 2 at 2792 on the June 2026 FIDE list; the second-highest peak rating in history and Carlsen's closest classical rival for a decade.
personas.styleDesc: The ultimate preparation machine: enormous, engine-deep opening files backed by cold, precise calculation. Plays principled main lines — Ruy Lopez and slow Italians with White, the ultra-solid Petroff with Black — and beats elite opponents by simply out-analyzing them 25 moves deep. When the position demands it he attacks with surgical, fully-calculated violence rather than intuition.

personas.name: Ding Liren
personas.bio: The 17th World Champion (2023), first Chinese man to hold the title, winning an epic tiebreak against Nepomniachtchi with the courageous Rg6 rook lift. Three-time Chinese champion and former world No. 2 whose 100-game unbeaten streak stood as a modern record; lost the crown to Gukesh in 2024.
personas.title: GM
personas.modernEloNote: The 2023-2024 world champion has slid to the low 2700s amid health struggles and a long slump, well below his 2816 peak and 100-move unbeaten-streak form.
personas.styleDesc: Deeply solid, harmonious positional player with world-class defensive resilience — he once went 100 classical games unbeaten. Prefers Catalan and English structures with White and airtight Nimzo/QGD setups with Black, but hides a spectacular creative streak: when the moment comes he produces immortal-level attacking sequences with quiet, impossible-looking moves. Endgame precision and nerve under pressure are his trademarks.

personas.name: Gukesh Dommaraju
personas.bio: The 18th and youngest-ever undisputed World Champion, defeating Ding Liren in Singapore 2024 at age 18 after winning the 2024 Candidates as its youngest victor. Chennai prodigy mentored in Viswanathan Anand's academy; anchored India's gold-medal Olympiad team in 2024 with a monster individual performance.
personas.title: GM
personas.modernEloNote: The reigning world champion sits in the world top ten around 2750-2770 — phenomenal for a teenager, though a notch below Carlsen's outright strength.
personas.styleDesc: Ferocious classical fighter with old-school ambition in every game: plays both 1.e4 and 1.d4, steers for rich, imbalanced middlegames, and simply refuses to take draws. His calculation depth and stamina in long grinding positions are extraordinary for his age, and his nerveless conversion of tiny endgame edges decided the world championship. As Black he counterattacks with the Najdorf and active piece play.

personas.name: Levy Rozman (GothamChess)
personas.bio: The world's most-watched chess educator: New York IM turned YouTube superstar 'GothamChess' with over six million subscribers, whose recaps and 'Guess the Elo' series brought millions into the game during the chess boom. A genuinely strong player, he beat future-GM Hans Niemann over the board and authored the bestseller 'How to Win at Chess'.
personas.title: IM
personas.modernEloNote: Peak FIDE 2421 as an active IM around 2018; now mostly retired from serious OTB (rated ~2320) so honest current strength is roughly 2350 against today's field.
personas.styleDesc: Aggressive club-crusher chess: 1.e4 with the Vienna Gambit as a calling card, plus Italian piece storms and Evans Gambit-style romantic play aimed straight at the enemy king. With Black he champions the Caro-Kann — solid structure, then a sudden counterpunch — and the King's Indian. Loves forcing lines, traps and initiative; will burn material for an open king every time.
